VO_EN: Your daughter asked what's for dinner.
The screen glows blue in the dark.
Your hand rests on a joystick.
The air conditioning hums. The trailer smells like dust and old coffee.
Ten thousand feet below, a village sleeps.
But you know the name of the man in the second house on the left.
You know when he wakes up.
You know when his children leave for school.
You know what he eats for breakfast and which route he takes to the mosque.
You know all of this because it's your job to know.
And one day, it'll be your job to make him stop.
You thought you'd feel the sky pull at your chest, the throttle shaking under your hand.
A trailer that could be anywhere.
And a war you commute to.
You sign up for the Air Force because you want to fly.
The recruiter shows you a poster - an F-16 cutting through clouds.
That's the word you keep coming back to.
They assign you to Remotely Piloted Aircraft.
You look it up that night in the barracks.
They don't call it that officially, but that's what it is.
The other cadets who got fighter assignments shake your hand like they're sorry.
One of them says it quietly.
At least you'll be home for dinner.
You don't know why yet, but that sentence is going to follow you for years.
Undergraduate pilot training, then the RPA pipeline, then MQ-9 Reaper qualification.
You learn to fly something you'll never sit inside.
Forty-five miles northwest of Las Vegas.
The heat makes the buildings shimmer.
Your cockpit is a windowless trailer with air conditioning that hums too loud.
Two seats. Twelve screens. A joystick. A throttle.
The chair is the same model they use in office supply stores.
Somewhere on the other side of the world, your aircraft is already circling.
You can see the ground from here.
Your wife picks you up after your first shift.
Your daughter's in the back seat. She's two.
She waves at you through the window.
You think you understand how this works.
You drive in at four AM and drive home at four PM.
It doesn't matter after a while. The days blur.
Somewhere above Afghanistan or Yemen or Somalia, your Reaper is watching.
You don't know his real name.
What you need is his pattern of life.
You watch him for six weeks.
You watch him walk his son to school.
You watch him pray at dawn, knees on a rug in the yard.
The camera resolution is good enough to see the color of the rug.
His youngest runs in circles while he prays.
She looks about the same age as yours.
You know what he cooks on Fridays.
You know his youngest likes to play in the front yard after the heat breaks.
You know when his wife hangs laundry and when she takes it in.
You've logged more hours watching this man than you've spent with your own family this month.
But you know everything about him.
You document every detail of a man's existence - his habits, his contacts, his family, the way he walks, who visits on Thursdays - all of it goes into a file.
The file gets thicker every week.
And one day someone above you will read it and decide he's done living.
Past the elementary school on Desert Inn Road.
Past the park where kids are playing.
The same age as the ones you watched today from the other side of the world.
Nobody warns you about the quiet parts.
The drive home is the worst.
Your wife asks how your day was.
Your son asks what you do for work.
You say dad works with computers.
He nods and goes back to his homework.
You stand in the kitchen for a second too long, looking at nothing.
Saturday your neighbor invites you to a barbecue.
His kids run through the sprinkler.
You can still see the compound from this morning behind your eyelids.
You sip the beer and nod at something the neighbor says.
The fighter pilots call you chair force.
Never felt your heart slam against your ribs at Mach one.
What you've done is sit in a chair for twelve hours and watch a man live his entire life - his breakfast, his prayers, his kid's laughter - so that when the order comes, you can end it.
The sensor operator who sits next to you is named Davis.
You've spent three thousand hours in a dark room together.
You've never been to his house.
You both just look at the same screen and don't talk about what you see.
One night you almost tell your wife.
I watched a man eat dinner with his family and wrote down what time he finished.
The military doctor will have a name for it later.
The rapid transition from lethal combat to domestic normalcy in the same day.
You don't know the term yet.
You just know you've been talking less.
But you don't know how to stop it.
You wipe your hands on your pants under the desk.
The AC in the trailer clicks off for a second and everything goes quiet.
You don't remember when you stopped eating lunch.
Your jaw is tight. You're not sure when that started.
Your shoulders are up near your ears.
JACKPOT is sitting in his yard.
The youngest is running circles around the table.
The one who plays in the front yard.
The one who looks like yours.
The one you've watched grow an inch since you started.
You see the child sit down next to his father.
Something in your chest skips. Not pain. Just a skip.
You're required to stay and watch.
You stay on target for twenty minutes.
The sensor operator beside you takes off his headset.
The radio plays a pop song you've heard a hundred times.
The receipt is warm in your hand.
Your daughter runs to your legs at the front door.
Same age as the one on the screen.
Your wife asks how your day was.
Every time you close your eyes you see the yard.
The next morning there's a new callsign.
You just load a new file.
You go back the next day.
You sit in the same chair.
It's easier when you stop counting.
You try to explain it to someone once.
A buddy from basic who flies C-130s.
He buys you a drink and listens.
But halfway through you realize he doesn't understand.
Because there's no version of this that makes sense out loud.
Your wife finds the bottle under the bathroom sink.
She says she doesn't recognize you anymore.
Because you don't recognize yourself either.
You leave it there. The bottle.
The military doctor asks if you've experienced combat.
Never heard a bullet pass your ear.
From a swivel chair in an air-conditioned trailer forty-five miles from Las Vegas.
Not combat. Not really. Not the way anyone uses that word.
You don't know what to call that.
The doctor writes something. You can't see what.
But the file reads non-combat related stress.
The infantry veteran sitting across from you in the VA waiting room lost both legs in Kandahar.
You find a job doing IT support for an insurance company.
Your first week, a coworker drops a filing cabinet.
The sound cracks through the office.
Your daughter calls once a week.
You want to tell her what you did.
Sometimes late at night you open Google Maps.
You zoom into villages you don't recognize.
You scroll around for a while.
You find a yard with a table.
Everything blurs for a second - the screen, the room, your hands on the trackpad - and then it comes back.
Another life lived from a chair.
But this time it's a laptop.
The desk is in your apartment.
No one is watching the screen but you.
No one on the other end.
And the only thing watching is you.
You look down at the keyboard.
You killed from ten thousand miles away.
And you made it home for dinner.
